---
title: "Analyzing Music Data"
author: "Suhani Shah"
format: html
execute:
  echo: false
---


```{r}
#| message: false
install.packages("tidyverse")
library(tidyverse)
billboard
```

```{r}
#| cache: true
billboard %>% 
  select(artist, track, date.entered, wk1:wk4)
summary(billboard$date.entered)

```

```{r}
#| cache: true
# Create the histogram and store the number of values used
n_values <- sum(!is.na(billboard$wk6))

hist(billboard$wk6, 
     main = paste("Histogram of Week 6 Rankings (n =", n_values, ")"),
     xlab = "Rankings", 
     col = "skyblue")

```

```{r}
#| cache: true
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

billboard %>%
  select(wk1, wk4, wk10, wk20, wk40, wk76) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(), names_to = "Week", values_to = "Rankings") %>%
  group_by(Week) %>%
  summarize(
    Present = sum(!is.na(Rankings)),
    Missing = sum(is.na(Rankings))
  )
```

```{r}
#| cache: true
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(stringr)

billboard %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("wk"), names_to = "week", values_to = "rank") %>%
  mutate(week = as.numeric(str_remove(week, "wk"))) %>%
  filter(!is.na(rank)) %>%
  group_by(artist, track) %>%
  summarize(
    has_gap = (max(week) - min(week) + 1) > n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  count(re_entered = has_gap)

```

```{r}
#| cache: true
library(tidyverse)

# Load and prepare the data
data("billboard")

# Process the data to compare wk1 and wk6
analysis <- billboard %>%
  select(track, wk1, wk6) %>%
  mutate(
    status = case_when(
      is.na(wk1) | is.na(wk6) ~ "Missing",
      wk6 < wk1 ~ "Improved",        # Lower number means higher rank
      wk6 > wk1 ~ "Got Worse",
      wk6 == wk1 ~ "Stayed the Same"
    ),
    rank_change = wk6 - wk1
  )

# 1. Count the statuses
status_counts <- analysis %>%
  count(status)

# 2. Calculate median rank change (excluding missing values)
median_change <- analysis %>%
  filter(!is.na(wk1) & !is.na(wk6)) %>%
  summarize(median_val = median(rank_change))

# Print results
print(status_counts)
print(median_change)

```

```{r}
#| cache: true
library(tidyverse)

# 1. Reshape the dataset from wide to long format
billboard_long <- billboard %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("wk"), 
    names_to = "week", 
    names_prefix = "wk",
    names_transform = list(week = as.numeric), # Convert week to a number for plotting
    values_to = "rank",
    values_drop_na = TRUE                      # Drop weeks where the song wasn't on the chart
  )

# 2. Plot each song's ranking over time
ggplot(billboard_long, aes(x = week, y = rank, group = track)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.2, color = "darkblue") + # Use transparency due to many overlapping lines
  scale_y_reverse() +                          # Reverse y-axis because rank 1 is the best
  labs(
    title = "Billboard Song Rankings Over Time",
    x = "Week",
    y = "Rank (1 is Top)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

```

```{r}
#| cache: true
library(tidyverse)

# 1. Ensure data is reshaped (from Exercise 9)
billboard_long <- billboard %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("wk"),
    names_to = "week",
    names_prefix = "wk",
    names_transform = list(week = as.numeric),
    values_to = "rank",
    values_drop_na = TRUE
  )

# 2. Create a song-level summary
song_summary <- billboard_long %>%
  group_by(artist, track) %>%
  summarize(
    first_rank = rank[week == min(week)],
    best_rank = min(rank),
    first_week_best = min(week[rank == best_rank]),
    total_weeks = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# 3. Filter for songs that reached the Top 10
top_10_songs <- song_summary %>%
  filter(best_rank <= 10)

# 4. Identify the three notable trajectories
fastest_no1 <- top_10_songs %>%
  filter(best_rank == 1) %>%
  slice_min(first_week_best, n = 1)

slowest_no1 <- top_10_songs %>%
  filter(best_rank == 1) %>%
  slice_max(first_week_best, n = 1)

longest_run <- top_10_songs %>%
  slice_max(total_weeks, n = 1)

# Print the final insights
print(fastest_no1)
print(slowest_no1)
print(longest_run)

```

```{r}
#| cache: true
library(tidyverse)

# 1. Reshape the dataset from wide to long format
billboard_long <- billboard %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("wk"),
    names_to = "week",
    names_prefix = "wk",
    names_transform = list(week = as.numeric),
    values_to = "rank",
    values_drop_na = TRUE
  )

# 2. Find tracks that peaked in the top 10
top_10_tracks <- billboard_long %>%
  group_by(track) %>%
  filter(min(rank) <= 10) %>%
  ungroup()

# 3. Define the three notable songs to highlight
highlight_tracks <- c("Independent Women Part I", "Higher") 
# Note: Creed's "Higher" satisfies both the longest run and slowest to #1 in standard 2000 data.
# If your specific dataset split them into three distinct songs, simply list all three here.

# Separate into background tracks and highlighted tracks
bg_data <- top_10_tracks %>% filter(!track %in% highlight_tracks)
fg_data <- top_10_tracks %>% filter(track %in% highlight_tracks)

# 4. Generate the plot
ggplot() +
  # Background layer: All top 10 songs in light gray
  geom_line(data = bg_data, aes(x = week, y = rank, group = track), color = "lightgray", alpha = 0.5) +
  # Foreground layer: Highlighted songs with distinct colors assigned to the aesthetic legend
  geom_line(data = fg_data, aes(x = week, y = rank, color = track, group = track), linewidth = 1.2) +
  # Format axes and design elements
  scale_y_reverse(limits = c(100, 1), breaks = c(1, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100)) + 
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(
    title = "Hit Trajectories of Top 10 Billboard Songs",
    subtitle = "Highlighting notable trajectories against all top 10 hits",
    x = "Week on Chart",
    y = "Rank",
    color = "Notable Songs"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

```
